segments.text:
Pentose phosphate pathway
shunt around the first stage of glycolysis that generates NADPH and Ribose-5-P
Glucose
Gluc 6P
Fruc 6P
Glycolysis
Pyruvate
G3P
NADP
CO 2
Ribose 5P
NADPH
Glutathione reduction (RBC)
Fatty acid/steroid synthesis
Nucleic acid
synthesis
Also known as HMP shunt
All in the cytoplasm
No ATP needed

All reactions are cytosolic: 2 phases:
Oxidative phase → NADPH
Non-oxidative phase → pentose sugars (used in the synthesis of nucleic acids)
The first enzyme of this pathway is glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase reduces NADP to NADPH
NADPH provides reducing equivalence for biosynthesis of FA or cholesterol
Pentose phosphate pathway

22.6 Pentose phosphate shunt: oxidative steps
Figure 22.22 The pentose phosphate pathway.  The numerals in the blue circles indicate the steps discussed in the text.

NAD vs NADP
Both are electron carriers
Difference is a phosphate group (non-functional)
Breakdown of hydrocarbons: NAD to NADH
NAD is regenerated by ETC
Biosynthesis of hydrocarbons (sugars, cholesterol, fatty acids, etc): NADPH to NADP
NADPH is regenerated by pentose phosphate pathway

The Oxidative Steps of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway
Figure 22.23 The glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase reaction is the committed step in the pentose phosphate pathway.

Figure 22.24 The Gluconolactonase Reaction:      ring opening
Figure 22.24 The gluconolactonase reaction.  The uncatalyzed reaction also occurs.

The Oxidative Steps of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway: 6-C sugar to 5-C sugar
Figure 22.25  The 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase reaction.  Initial NADP + -dependent dehydrogenation yields a β -keto acid, 3-keto-6-phosphogluconate, which is very susceptible to decarboxyation (the second step).  The resulting product, D -ribulose-5-P, is the substrate for the nonoxidative reactions of the pentose phosphate pathway.

The Nonoxidative Steps of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway
Figure 22.26 The phosphopentose isomerase reaction converts a ketose to an aldose.  The reaction involves an enediol intermediate.

Rate limiting step in P.P. pathway
Produces NADPH from NADP
NADPH is used to make FAs and Cholesterol
NADPH is used by glutathione reductase to reduce glutathione
Reduced glutathione converts H 2 O 2 to H 2 0
Glucose 6-P Dehydrogenase
Antioxidant

X-linked recessive
Increases resistance to malaria
Common among individuals of African descent
Results in RBC hemolysis
Glucose 6-P Dehydrogenase deficiency

Utilization of Glucose-6-P Depends on the Cell ’ s Need for ATP, NADPH, and Rib-5-P
Glucose can be a substrate either for glycolysis or for the pentose phosphate pathway
The choice depends on the relative needs of the cell for biosynthesis and for energy from metabolism
ATP can be made if G-6-P is sent to glycolysis
Or, if NADPH or ribose-5-P are needed for biosynthesis, G-6-P can be directed to the pentose phosphate pathway
